description: Figure 8 presents two quantitative line-graph panels that correlate microstructural parameters with hot-rolling reduction in a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W (wt%) austenitic low-density steel. Panel (a) plots the mean grain size excluding twins, the effective grain size including twin boundaries, and the dislocation density versus rolling reduction (45%, 68%, and 82%). The grain size excluding twins decreases from approximately 45 μm to 19 μm, the effective grain size including twins drops from roughly 22 μm to 7 μm, and the geometrically necessary dislocation density rises from about 2.7×10¹³ m⁻² to 9.9×10¹³ m⁻² as reduction increases. Panel (b) displays the volume fractions and mean particle sizes of κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C precipitates. The κ-carbide volume fraction remains in the range of ~3.3–3.9% with a mean size near 0.8 nm, while the (Nb,Mo,W)C volume fraction stays around 0.2% with a mean size of ~260 nm, both nearly unchanged with increasing reduction. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that higher hot-rolling reduction refines austenite grains and raises dislocation density without appreciably altering precipitate characteristics, supporting the core conclusion that the observed strength increment stems primarily from grain-boundary and dislocation strengthening rather than from precipitate strengthening.
